import numpy as np

The simplest case: first-order differential equation

children.label: The simplest case: first-order differential equation

children.value: is capable of solving first-order differential equation of the form

children.html: y˙=f(t,y)\dot{y} = f(t, y)y˙​=f(t,y)
y0=y(t0)y_0 = y(t_0)y0​=y(t0​)

children.value: is given. As an explicit example, consider the logistic differential equation:

children.html: dPdt=rP(1−PK)\frac{dP}{dt} = r P \left( 1 - \frac{P}{K} \right)dtdP​=rP(1−KP​)

is also capable of solving a system of first-order differential equations. Conceptually, all we are doing is to promote

y˙\dot{y}y˙​

children.value: ) from a scalar to a 1D array. As before, we need to put the system of equation into a standard form

children.html: y˙=f(t,y)\dot{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{f}(t, \mathbf{y})y˙​=f(t,y)
y\mathbf{y}y
y˙1=f1(t,y1,y2)y˙2=f2(t,y1,y2)\begin{align} \dot{y}_1 & = f_1(t, y_1, y_2) \\ \dot{y}_2 & = f_2(t, y_1, y_2) \end{align}y˙​1​y˙​2​​=f1​(t,y1​,y2​)=f2​(t,y1​,y2​)​​

children.value: As an explicit example, consider the Lotka-Volterra Predator-Prey Model. The system is (with

children.html: xxx
yyy
dxdt=αx−βxydydt=−γy+δxy\begin{align} \frac{dx}{dt} & = \alpha x - \beta x y \\ \frac{dy}{dt} & = -\gamma y + \delta x y \end{align}dtdx​dtdy​​=αx−βxy=−γy+δxy​​

children.value:
def predator_prey(t, Y):
    return [
        alpha * Y[0] - beta * Y[0] * Y[1],
        -gamma * Y[1] + delta * Y[0] * Y[1]
    ]

alpha = 24
beta = 0.4
gamma = 2.0
delta = 0.002

t_span = [0, 5]
Y0 = [3000, 50]

out = sint.solve_ivp(predator_prey, t_span, Y0, dense_output=True)

t_array = np.linspace(0, 5, 200)
Y_array = out.sol(t_array)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(t_array, Y_array[0], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")
ax.plot(t_array, Y_array[1], c="tab:orange", ls="--", label="predator")

plt.show(fig)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(Y_array[0], Y_array[1], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")

plt.show(fig)

# switch to an implicit solver
out2 = sint.solve_ivp(predator_prey, t_span, Y0, method="Radau", dense_output=True)

t_array = np.linspace(0, 5, 200)
Z_array = out2.sol(t_array)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot()

ax.plot(Z_array[0], Z_array[1], c="tab:blue", ls="-", label="prey")

plt.show(fig)
Cumulative exercise: van der Pol oscillator

children.label: Cumulative exercise: van der Pol oscillator

children.value: The van der Pol oscillator has been used to model cardiac activities. It is a second-order ODE of the form:

children.html: d2xdt2−μ(1−x2)dxdt+x=0\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} - \mu (1-x^{2}) \frac{dx}{dt} + x = 0dt2d2x​−μ(1−x2)dtdx​+x=0
μ=1.5\mu = 1.5μ=1.5

children.value: . Solve the ODE for a variety of initial condition

children.html: (x(0),x˙(0))\left(x(0), \dot{x}(0)\right)(x(0),x˙(0))
(x(t),x˙(t))(x(t), \dot{x}(t))(x(t),x˙(t))

children.value: does not directly come with a higher-order ODE solver. However, there is a standard trick that convert a higher-order ODE into a system of first-order ODE. At second order the trick amounts to setting

children.html: y=x˙y = \dot{x}y=x˙

children.value: , which in itself becomes one of the equations of the system, while the original differential equation becomes an equation of the form

children.html: y˙=f(x,y,t)\dot{y} = f(x, y, t)y˙​=f(x,y,t)
